data.title: A SpaceX-Tesla Merger Would Likely Destroy Value For Tesla Shareholders
data.description:
The last time I wrote about Tesla, Inc. (TSLA), I had this line which still rings true.

The core business is in trouble, so look at this shiny new thing instead.

Today that shiny new thing is the speculation around a Tesla - SpaceX (SPACE) merger. As I highlight in this article, there are clear operational synergies for such a transaction. But those synergies do not justify the cost in dilution, governance risks, or a probable conglomerate discount. Given the punishing math for Tesla shareholders I am persisting with my Sell on the stock.

A Small Valuation Gap and a Treacherous Exchange Ratio

The trajectory of SpaceX’s valuation has been quite extraordinary. It went from being valued at $210 billion in June 2024 to $1.25 trillion at the end of January 2026. That is a 5.95x multiplier in roughly 20 months. The thing about private market valuations is that they tend to get inflated by a closed group of sophisticated participants who can’t sell easily. Because of the absence of continuous price discovery and presence of restricted exit options, valuations have likely drifted more upwards than they deserve. Such a dynamic matters in the context of a merger.

For instance, in a hypothetical all-stock merger at current valuations, Tesla shareholders would retain about 55% of the merged entity ($1,570 / $2,820). If you consider some of the figures from Reuters and Bloomberg, this would effectively fold an entity trading ~78 times sales, materially richer than Tesla's own revenue multiple.

On the institutional side, investors will likely respond cautiously even if the strategic narrative is compelling. An entity operating in electric vehicles, rockets, satellites, and AI will introduce conglomerate complexity. Markets tend to discount such structures especially when capital intensity is high and regulatory exposure is significant. So it is possible you have a merger that imports a premium valuation in the beginning and then invites multiple compression across the combined entity as the narrative flows through.

Lastly because of Tesla's engineering ties with China and SpaceX's contracts with the Department of War, a merger would likely trigger a review by the CFIUS. Senators Elizabeth Warren and Andy Kim have raised questions in this regard in a recent letter to Peter Hegseth, the Secretary of War. I think these aren't likely to fade as there are enough China hawks on both sides of the political aisle. A structural remedy is a forced divestiture of Tesla's business in China and that would further limit the company's growth profile.

Misaligned Incentives and the Bylaw Shield

I think the potential for this deal also stems in part from the misalignment of incentives between CEO Elon Musk and minority shareholders. If you consider that he owns roughly 18% of Tesla and 42% of SpaceX, Elon Musk's economic interest in the combined entity would be around 29%.

($ billion) Tesla SpaceX Combined
Market Cap 1,570 1,250 2,820
% interest 18.00% 42.00% 28.64%
Interest in $ billions 282.60 525.00 807.60

Tesla's new CEO compensation plan is linked to market cap milestones. This creates a powerful incentive to fold SpaceX into Tesla. But does it benefit the minority shareholder? I doubt it. After the adoption of a bylaw, Tesla requires a shareholder to own at least 3% of the company to file a lawsuit against the breach of fiduciary duty. That is around $42 billion in stock holdings, making a challenge unlikely.

To be Fair Synergies Exist

To give credit where it is due, Tesla has a history where improvement in one company's technology ends up benefitting others. Chief among these examples are the application of 30X stainless steel which was developed for the Starship program and then applied to the Tesla Cybertruck. That goes to show that technological spillover's aren't just theoretical.

Extending that logic, Tesla's visual training data from millions of vehicles can be used to train xAI's spatial reasoning models. These can then be used to automate SpaceX's landing and docking procedures. On paper this does seem like an elegant loop of shared intelligence. But the issue is less about whether it would happen. It is whether these will unfold over decades rather than quarters. When the horizon is that far out, investors hesitate and wait for clearer milestones and may be a more attractive entry point.

Conclusion

While TSLA stock is up slightly since the news, it hasn't been on a tear like the way you would have seen a few years ago. I think that suggests the Street is looking at this with caution. Especially for the risk-averse investor this scenario is a minefield of legal and financial dangers. I am therefore persisting with my Sell on this stock.

data.title: Talisker Resources: Heavily Rerated (~500% Up) But Still Undervalued
data.description:
Talisker Resources (TSKFF) (TSK:CA), founded in 1987, is a Canadian junior mining company in the ramp-up phase and owns a historically famous high-grade gold camp located in British Columbia. The company is headquartered in Toronto, Canada, and it has a market cap of ~US$260 million.

I am initiating coverage with a Strong Buy rating. Talisker owns a rare, historically proven high-grade district in Bralorne that has started to produce and is now in the transition to monetization with significant short-term milestones that should allow for further derisking.

Even after a ~500% rally in the last 12 months, valuation remains attractive, partly because the current resource base is largely inferred, which signals low confidence. Ongoing conversion drilling and an updated NI 43-101 document expected this year provide the condition and the catalyst needed to keep the market rerating the stock.

Portfolio & Management

Talisker's core asset is the Bralorne Gold Project, a district-scale land package located in southwestern British Columbia that contained various historic mines in the previous century and the currently active Mustang, an underground mine. The land covers ~14K hectares and has a mineralized trend of ~33km, which is unusual for junior exploration projects.

The company bought the land from Avino Silver & Gold Mines Ltd. back in 2019, and it is the main reason Talisker has attracted capital since then. Bralorne is famous for 47 mineral occurrences/historic mines, which implies a strong pattern of mineralizing events. This is important because it means this is more about connecting the dots than starting blind.

Historically (1897 to 1971), mining in Bralorne has produced a total of 4.2 million ounces of gold at an average grade of ~17.7 grams per tonne of ore (g/t), which is extremely high even for underground mines (5-8 g/t is average). Operations ended back in 1971 because costs kept rising while gold was extremely cheap.

As for Bralorne's current resource base, the latest NI 43-101 technical report filed in March 2023 shows that it consists of ~117,300 indicated tonnes at an 8.85 g/t grade and ~8.03 million inferred tonnes at 6.32 g/t (a total of 1,666,300 oz Au). An indicated resource is based on denser drilling and sampling data, while an inferred resource relies more on geological evidence that suggests continuity and is, therefore, more uncertain. Notice that Talisker doesn't have any measured resource, which is based on a data density high enough for continuity to be practically confirmed, and the overwhelming majority of the resource base is inferred.

Bralorne is the most promising project, but it's not the only one. Talisker also has the Ladner Gold Project, which encompasses the Carolin mine, the McMaster zone, and tailings totaling more (mostly inferred) ore at ~15.8 million tonnes but less gold at 691,540 oz, implying a much lower grade. Obviously, Ladner is not a priority, and nor should it be.

Last, the Greenfields pipeline is 266,654 hectares, which encompasses the Spences Bridge Gold Project (126,720 hectares). This asset is arguably one step ahead of typical greenfield assets because the company controls more than 85% of the belt. This is a huge hunting zone and provides significant optionality; a discovery can help re-rate the shares. It certainly helps that the search area is so large because a discovery will imply scaling potential; this cannot be said for every junior out there.

As for the management team, it is led by Terence Harbort (CEO), who is a structural geologist (PhD) and has decades of experience in the industry. He is credited with being a part of discovery teams at AngloGold Ashanti and is a former chief geologist at Barkerville Gold Mines. The Vice President is Leonardo de Souza, who has over 35 years of experience in mineral exploration and 21 years of experience in mineral resource estimation. Andres Tinajero, the CFO, is a CPA with over 30 years of experience and has had various roles (VP, CFO, corporate controller) for large multinational companies.

Financials

For the nine months ended September 30, 2025, Talisker reported 15,266 tonnes of ore and 22,826 tonnes of waste mined; the 1.5:1 ratio here is high but not unusual for this stage and is expected to normalize with scaling. Additionally, it processed 13,481 tonnes, stockpiled 1,118 tonnes, and produced 1,596 oz of gold.

The mining rate was 177 tonnes per day, and the cost ended up being C$402 per tonne, which is generally high but typical for the ramp-up phase. Predictably, Talisker is not profitable yet; it generated its first revenue of C$5.45 million in the third quarter of 2025, and its processing cost for the 9-month period was C$6.82 million, realizing a gross margin of -C$1.37 million.

According to its latest presentation, as of October 2025, Talisker had about C$33 million in cash, a C$35 million revolving credit facility, and minimal debt at ~C$1.6 million outstanding. While that financial situation seems attractive, the cash burn is high. Talisker incurred net losses of C$5.03 million and C$4.61 million in Q1 and Q2 of 2025, respectively. Operating cash outflow was C$10.4 million, and cash used for development was C$8.7 million in the first 9 months of last year. This ~C$19 million burn was funded by ~C$21 million of equity financing. Indeed, the share count increased from 98.3 million as of November 2024 to 177.692 million as of November 2025.

Now, the high institutional ownership of the stock is a positive signal, as you can reasonably assume there is confidence in the prospects based on geological and jurisdictional due diligence. Institutional investors probably see the path to production increases and monetization as likely and are still willing to tolerate development risk even after the price surged by that much. The 5% insider ownership is also signaling meaningful skin in the game, so an intent for empire-building and reckless dilution is unlikely here.

Performance

The pandemic, the loss of momentum in the rising price of gold, and the risk-off period that came in 2022 help explain why that high level reached in 2020 was not sustained. You also have to take into account the idiosyncratic factor of an increase in operating expenses that came with the purchase of the Bralorne project; the timing wasn't good.

Of course, as you may already be aware, the price surged again recently. In the last 12 months, TSKFF climbed by ~500%. That is consistent with the sequence of de-risking events that occurred in 2025.

First of all, in January, the company announced it hired a third-party mining contractor to begin mobilization and pre-operation work on the Bralorne mine. That included a Mustang mine plan to be reviewed with the British Columbia Ministry of Mines and Critical Minerals.

Later, in May, the company renewed a milling agreement with Nicola Mining Inc. and started transporting mine material to Nicola’s Craigmont Mill. Naturally, this signaled even more strongly that revenue generation isn't so far away anymore.

Additionally, in the second quarter, Talisker announced that it raised ~C$22 million, which lowers the risk that funding is going to be an issue.

There are two important re-rating triggers, however, that pointed to monetization. The first was the July 30 announcement that the company entered into an agreement with Ocean Partners for the purchase of gravity gold and sulfide concentrate from Mustang. Second, what helped the stock price keep the momentum it developed was the "first revenue" milestone that it reached in August 2025 with its first gold sale at 707 ounces. Both of those events shift the risk from whether they can sell gold or not to how fast they can scale and how efficiently.

In late October, Talisker also announced that it was in the process of signing an ore purchase agreement with Ocean Partners, which is part of the scaling plan. This offtake agreement effectively replaces the classic toll-milling structure with Nicola (the agreement expires in May) and reduces the risk of not finding a buyer at this stage.

Last, in January 2026, the company released more high-grade drill results as part of its efforts to increase the confidence level of its inferred resources (what we discussed above).

And, yes, the exceptional gold rally was an important factor for sentiment as well.

Now, the chart below will help you see how important new developments are for TSKFF. The price entered a long consolidation phase late last year before finally breaking through its 50DMA (blue line), testing it, and finally exploding through its upper Bollinger band (set at 2.5σ) in January 2026 (correlating with the last catalyst mentioned above). In February, it retested the 50DMA and then reversed, bringing its RSI above 50 again.

But the big picture is that it preserved the widening between the 50DMA and 200DMA (orange line), which started developing back in the summer, when the company first announced the purchase agreement with Ocean Partners and later its first gold sale. This indicates a consistency in the rerating process.

Catalysts & Risks

Obviously, the market is appreciating the stock but requires ongoing progress. And 2026 has a couple of key catalysts that should be very impactful on market sentiment.

First, Talisker is planning to amend its production permit for Bralorne from its current 175 tonnes per day rate to 500 tpd, which is a significant change in throughput and should reanchor expectations about revenue. The process has already been started, and Talisker has said it has received indications from the British Columbia Ministry of Mining and Critical Minerals that it should get the approval by the third quarter. Permits don't imply realization, of course. But consider that the historic mine complex ran at a range of ~136 to 500 tpd. We're talking about the period 1928-1971, when the technology was so far behind. Today, scaling is constrained more by permitting standards than by engineering. Therefore, the amendment is a crucial milestone to watch out for.

Second, on January 7, Talisker announced they have engaged SGS Canada Inc. to deliver a NI 43-101 technical report with an updated MRE (Mineral Resource Estimate) and PEA (Preliminary Economic Assessment). This is expected earlier in the second quarter, and the update will include 138 additional drill holes and the depletion that occurred in 2025 so the resource base can be updated. It will also report the expansion criteria for the Mustang mine and the Olympus mine concept, which involves a second underground mine in Bralorne and implies a potential increase in output and a decrease in operational risk (diversification).

The MRE (updating the drill holes and depletion to update the size and classification of the resource base) is as important as the PEA in this case, and here's why. Most of the drilling since 2022 (the latest MRE) was part of the "resource conversion" program rather than production efforts. The priority shifted towards production only recently (2025), so the depletion is probably going to be minor compared to the addition. Growth, if any, shouldn't be meaningful, but that's the case for a potential shrinkage as well. The key is in the potential for a meaningful reclassification of the resource base, and the case for it is strong for exactly the same reason that the size of the base probably hasn't changed too much: drilling efforts have focused on resource-conversion infill, which can convert the inferred resources to indicated. So, taking also into account that most of it has been reported based on a database close-out on October 3, 2022, and that most of it is inferred, the conversion potential is significant here.

The short-term risks are tied to some of these expectations, of course. While I have no reason to believe that the market prices in the likelihood of a significant conversion potential later this year (see valuation section), if the permit amendment is delayed, it is likely that the market will express some disappointment.

Additionally, "500 tpd permit" doesn't imply "500 tpd capacity." If market expectations are for Talisker to soon reach this level, this becomes a short-term meaningful risk. It's also not unreasonable to expect that a lot of the current enthusiasm anchors on the permit catalyst because it implies scaling potential, which is the obvious challenge after finding a buyer. "Verbal indications" are good to have, but they imply a high degree of uncertainty.

Some longer-term risks to monitor now. First, the dilution risk is always non-trivial for micro/small-cap stocks. As I already mentioned above, the stock has already been significantly diluted lately as Talisker transitioned to its ramp-up phase. That is normal, of course, as at this stage, debt financing is very expensive. Case in point: they have issued a 12% unsecured convertible debenture and 15% unsecured gold-linked notes in the past. Still, the undrawn C$35M credit facility could help slow down dilution until Talisker becomes self-funding.

Another risk that applies to the stage Talisker is in is execution; the important milestones have been reached and a meaningful degree of derisking has occurred, but transitioning from explorer to developer can be a lengthy and expensive process. Depending upon investment horizons, this can represent an opportunity risk.

Lastly, the price of gold is another source of risk. Though the ore grade that we could reasonably expect here is above average and provides a bottom-line margin of safety, the market can re-risk shares on the basis of losing momentum or, worse, a fall in gold prices in the meantime.

Valuation

Because of the early stage of revenue generation this company is in and the idiosyncratic valuation metrics used for this industry, I have to note that the current P/E, P/B, and P/S ratios are not useful. So, at this stage, it's more appropriate to use Enterprise Value to Oz of gold.

A relevant peer is Blue Lagoon Resources (BLAGF), which is also a British Columbia high-grade underground junior in the ramp-up phase and outsources processing. As of the time of writing, its EV is C$144.4 million, and it has 234K ounces of gold based on its latest NI 43-101 document, resulting in C$615 per Oz.

Talisker has an EV of C$315.5 million, and based on its 1.6 million ounces of gold in Bralorne, the ratio is C$189 per Oz. But there is a catch. Blue Lagoon's base consists mostly of indicated and measured, rather than inferred, gold. In fact, the inferred portion is only 6.8% and the indicated is 73.9% of the total, while for Talisker, the inferred portion is the overwhelming majority, as I showed above. The market seems to be assigning a lower risk to Blue Lagoon's base because of this.

But there's one key difference that is still not appreciated enough here. Talisker's Bralorne project has a much richer history than Blue Lagoon's Dome Mountain Mine. Bralorne had produced ~4.2 million ounces at an average grade of around 17.7 g/t before it closed in 1971. And as I already said, it closed because of the very low gold price back then, not because the system ran out. A very important detail when discussing current conditions.

Blue Lagoon's Dome Mountain has historically produced only ~17,000 ounces at ~12 g/t Au. And yet its valuation is richer, indicating a high confidence in its prospects, probably because of the large portion of gold resources that is "indicated."

I'm not saying that there isn't a big leap from inferred to indicated. But I'll argue that after taking into account the richer history of Bralorne and the conversion potential in 2026 as discussed above, this specific valuation differential here is not warranted. The market is undervaluing TSKFF, but the current momentum, as seen above, suggests that it's on a path to adjust the price to a fairer level later this year.

Red Cloud Securities, a mining consultant and an underwriter for Talisker, initiated coverage in January and suggested a C$4.75 target, based on a DCF valuation. Although this should be taken with a grain of salt, I appreciate that the price target is discounted by 50% to account for the risk in execution.

Conclusion

Taking into account all that I discussed, the prospects outweigh the risks, and the valuation provides a margin of safety significant enough to warrant a position.

Different needs, different perspectives, though. I'd love to learn your opinion on Talisker. Are you a shareholder, or are you still considering a position? Leave a comment below, and I'll get back to you shortly!

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: XLE: A Fund Whose Time Has Come
data.description:
The State Street Energy Sector Select ETF (XLE) is a fund whose time has come. After nearly a decade out of favor—interrupted by one bull run in 2022—oil is finally showing signs of stabilizing and even rising, and the big oil majors are rising right along with it. After a brief period of hiking oil output, OPEC is now staying put, while generative AI is consuming ever-rising amounts of energy. Despite the rise of renewables, oil demand has been slowly increasing with time, and now, energy-intensive AI applications are providing a totally new source of demand for coal and natural gas. It all points to a healthy market for petrochemicals for some time in the future.

All of this is very bullish for XLE over the long term. Although oil is cyclical and vulnerable to recessions, the secular forces in play argue for continued demand growth until about 2050. Supply, while being strong at the moment, is expected to grow at a slower pace from 2027 onward. Again, this fact set argues for long-term strength despite potential soft pricing in 2026.

One factor that could help energy stocks in the decades ahead is generative artificial intelligence [AI]. The MIT Technology Review cited studies that claimed 4.4% of the energy used in the U.S. last year went to data centers, and data center usage is expected to rise in the decade ahead, driven largely by AI. While the big tech companies are publicly claiming that they are committed to using clean energy in their data centers, it’s a fact that many of them still run on fossil fuels, through both local electric grids and their own supplies.

That’s not to say that high oil prices are absolutely required for XLE to perform well. The ETF does have some midstream companies, which tend to lock in stable baseline fees over periods of decades—occasionally with incentive fees for when oil prices are high. These companies don’t “need” high oil prices to remain profitable, though extremely low prices can result in solvency issues for their clients, and high ones may result in somewhat higher fees. The point is midstream energy companies’ earnings and dividends don’t have the same immediate response to oil prices that E&Ps earnings do.

So, although XLE is pretty focused on E&Ps, it has the potential to make money in varying different market conditions. This, combined with the renewed strength in oil prices, provides for an ETF that could deliver serious alpha, particularly today, with tech stocks trading at inflated valuations and burning through unprecedented amounts of cash chasing the dream of artificial general intelligence [AGI]. For this reason, I consider the XLE ETF a buy today.

Oil Prices: Where They’ve Been, Where They’re Headed

Since many companies in XLE’s portfolio are integrated energy companies and E&Ps, it’s important to know where oil prices are heading. This topic is potentially a complex one because many countries around the world are pushing cleaner alternatives to oil & gas, including renewables and nuclear. If renewables and nuclear are going to eat oil’s lunch, then shouldn’t oil prices fall over the long term?

Not necessarily.

Global energy demand continues to grow and is expected to grow at 3.5% CAGR until 2030. Currently, only 30% of the world’s power comes from renewable sources. That’s expected to reach “nearly half” by 2030. Although the growth in renewables is expected to outpace growth in energy demand and oil demand, the 3.5% CAGR growth expected for the whole industry still leaves some room for oil demand to grow.

The next part of the equation is the supply side. Here, opinions differ. The International Energy Agency [IEA] sees supply growing faster than demand, resulting in a glut by 2030. OPEC, on the other hand, sees healthy growth in demand driving growth in supply, leading to a balanced market. Only time will tell which of these two forecasts is right; however, the rise in oil prices seen in the first half of 2026 is more consistent with OPEC’s forecast than IEA’s.

It’s also worth mentioning the massive increase in spending on AI data centers that’s coming in the next few years. All the big U.S. tech companies have guided for massive growth in AI capital expenditure [CAPEX] over the next 12 months, with total spending across the five biggest players expected to reach $673 billion in 2026. The “five biggest players” just mentioned do not include notably large players such as Tesla (TSLA), XAI, or any of the Chinese cloud companies.

The data centers just mentioned use massive amounts of energy, and while some of them have managed to procure renewable sources of energy, others have not. XAI, for example, is using natural gas power to train Grok. The amount of energy consumed by data centers is going to increase in the years ahead. If the scale of CAPEX is any indication, then the IEA may have underestimated future demand growth, in which case oil prices may play out in a manner more consistent with OPEC’s forecast than IEA’s. In that scenario, XLE should perform pretty well.

Top XLE Stocks: Valuation

Having established that oil prices are likely to remain reasonably strong in the near to medium term, we can now move on to valuation metrics. Given that XLE is an ETF, it’s not realistic to do a complete DCF valuation of holdings here; however, we can look at the multiples of its top five holdings. These are:

  • Exxon Mobil (XOM).

  • Chevron (CVX).

  • ConocoPhillips (COP).

  • SLB (SLB).

  • The Williams Companies Inc. (WMB)

In addition to the above stats from XLE’s top five stocks, here are the whole portfolio’s P/E ratio, price/book ratio, and forward earnings growth rate courtesy of State Street:

  • P/E: 19.8.

  • Price/book: 2.3.

  • Price/cash flow: 9.45.

  • EPS growth: 6%.

And here are some similar metrics for the S&P 500:

  • P/E: 23.6.

  • Price/book: 5.15.

  • Price/cash flow: 19.

  • EPS growth: 14%.

As you can see, XLE trades at lower multiples than the broader U.S. markets. However, it also has much less growth. Whereas the biggest XLE stocks saw their earnings decline last year and are expected to see them rise only 6% going forward, the S&P 500 has been growing its earnings and is expected to keep growing them at 14% CAGR. Ultimately, this means that the SPY has a lower PEG ratio than XLE has. However, there are serious concerns about whether the big AI companies (which make up the bulk of the S&P 500’s top 10) can actually hit their earnings forecasts long term, when they are projected to spend $700 billion on AI-related CAPEX in 2026. Their free cash flow is already declining, and their earnings will start getting hit by heavy depreciation in the next 12 months. Given that this is the case, I think that the SPY components will probably grow their earnings less than analysts are expecting in the year ahead and that XLE will prove to be a comparably good value in the period.

Other Fund Characteristics

In addition to trading at comparatively low multiples, XLE has some other attractive characteristics that are worth mentioning. These include:

  • A 2.7% portfolio dividend yield, more than double that of the S&P 500.

  • 22 stocks, a reasonable amount of diversification for a sector fund.

  • A very low 0.08% management fee.

  • A narrow 0.02% bid-ask spread.

Broadly, these characteristics suggest that XLE is inexpensive (in the sense of not paying too much of your money to fund managers and market makers), diversified, and with above-average near-term income potential.

One Risk to Keep in Mind

Despite the fact that I am overall bullish on XLE, I do recognize one very serious risk factor for the fund:

Intermittent weakness in oil prices.

I think oil prices will hold up reasonably well over the very long term. However, they can be quite volatile, and it’s not at all uncommon for them to be below many companies' breakeven for years. A good example was the COVID-19 lockdown period of 2020-early 2021, when many XLE components were unprofitable for the better part of a year.

The Bottom Line

The bottom line on XLE is that it is a long-unloved fund whose time has come. Owning it is a great way to get some oil & gas exposure, as well as some passive income, into your portfolio. With oil prices being surprisingly resilient and generative AI providing a new demand catalyst, you’ll be wanting oil & gas exposure. Owing to its 0.08% management fee, adequate diversification, and a narrow bid-ask spread, XLE might just be the place to get it.

data.title: Take Two Interactive Software: Strong Bookings Prospects Amid Selloff (Upgrade)
data.description:
Over the past few months, all manner of software stocks have been hammered, as suddenly investors are no longer enticed by recurring revenue streams and the high margins that these businesses provide, preferring instead to bet their chips on highly in-demand semiconductor stocks that are directly feeding the AI data center boom.

I caution investors not to follow the crowd too closely, especially as the recent selloff has positioned many previously expensive stocks at much more reasonable prices. Even gaming companies, such as Take Two Software (TTWO), have been heavily impacted, and the stock is down over 20% from December peaks above $260.

I last wrote a "Sell" article on Take Two in January, when the stock was trading at $250. At the time, I had argued that the stock commanded too rich a valuation premium, especially in the wake of a highly competitive gaming industry with platforms like Roblox (RBLX) gaining substantially in popularity. While I don’t necessarily think Take Two is a buy just yet, the recent drawdown in its valuation makes it a much more interesting play, especially amid a recent burst in bookings that drove a bump to full-year bookings expectations. We’re also aware of Take Two’s very strong pipeline ahead for fiscal FY27, which makes me more confident in upping my rating on the stock to "Neutral."

In my view, at current share prices, I see a more balanced bull and bear case for Take Two. On the bright side for the company:

  • Diverse portfolio of games to span interests and gaming levels. Take Two has an incredibly broad range of titles, spanning from casual racing and puzzle apps built for mobile, to sporting titles (NBA2k26), and the genre-bending Grand Theft Auto franchise, which will get its latest installment in late 2026.
  • Recurring and license sales fuel consistent bookings growth. Unlike traditional gaming companies, which primarily earn one-time revenue from game sales at launch, Take Two’s portfolio of apps and selection of in-game passes and purchases has also created a healthy recurring revenue stream that is also growing at a double-digit y/y pace.

At the same time, however, we are aware of a number of risks:

  • Casual gaming has no shortage of competitors. Some of the most popular games in 2025 were designed not by professional game studios, but simply by users and enthusiasts on the Roblox platform. AI is also leveling the playing field, enabling creatives and designers without technical chops to also create their own games at a fraction of the cost of a studio.
  • Reliance on M&A to achieve growth. Historically, Take Two has leveraged its balance sheet to gain market share in the >$200 billion annual gaming market, as evidenced by the company’s splashy acquisition of Zynga. It has continually snapped up smaller game studios every year, recently spending $460 million to buy a studio called Gearbox (home of the Borderlands franchise). The company is now in a slight net debt position, and investors may eventually sour on the company’s “buy rather than build” approach.

What ultimately keeps me on the sidelines for Take Two, however, is that despite the recent valuation meltdown, Take Two remains decently expensive. At current share prices near $200, Take Two trades at a market cap of $37.76 billion. Netting off the $2.36 billion of cash and $3.07 billion of debt on Take Two’s most recent balance sheet gives us an enterprise value of $38.47 billion.

For the remainder of FY26, Take Two has boosted its outlook to $6.65-$6.70 billion, a 3% bump from $6.40-$6.50 billion previously. It’s also now expecting meaningfully higher adjusted EBITDA at $657-$681 million ($669 million midpoint, or a 10.0% margin on bookings and 10% higher than previously).

For next year FY27 (the year ending in March 2027), Wall Street is currently expecting Take Two to deliver $9.18 billion in bookings, or 37% y/y growth. As a reminder, the main driver here is the expected November contribution from Grand Theft Auto VI, a franchise that has not had a mainline update in years. If we assume a similar 10% margin next year, adjusted EBITDA in FY27 would be $918 million. This positions Take Two’s valuation multiples at:

  • 42x EV/FY27 adjusted EBITDA
  • 57x EV/FY26 adjusted EBITDA

To this end, I do think strength is already priced into this stock and caps further upside. For now, stay on the sidelines here and invest elsewhere.

Q3 download

That said, we will acknowledge that even before Grand Theft Auto VI comes into the picture, Take Two’s existing slate is already driving tremendous performance for the company. Take a look at the company’s fiscal Q3 (December quarter) results below:

Take Two’s revenue grew 25% y/y to $1.70 billion, beating Wall Street’s expectations of $1.58 billion (+16% y/y) by a wide nine-point margin. Meanwhile, bookings grew at an even faster 28% y/y pace to $1.76 billion. As a reminder, when it comes to games that are supported by in-app revenue and not upfront license sales, consistent with other gaming companies, Take Two recognizes bookings first (when customers actually make a purchase). Revenue is then recognized as time passes or as items are used up. As such, strong bookings are a good indicator of the company’s strong future revenue trajectory.

The chart below gives good orientation as to Take Two’s recently released and upcoming slate. Recent anchor releases in the past few months include NBA2K26, as well as new Red Dead Redemption and Borderlands releases:

Online, recurring spending from the company's NBA2K26 and Grand Theft Auto online titles drove the bookings success for the quarter. Per CEO Strauss Zelnick's remarks on the Q3 earnings call:

NBA 2K26 delivered another stellar quarter, yielding significant upside to our forecast. To date, the title has sold-in approximately 8 million units, representing a high single-digit percentage increase over NBA 2K25. Recurrent consumer spending, daily active users and MyCAREER daily active users all grew 30% year-over-year. Based on its phenomenal year-to-date performance, NBA 2K is on track to generate the highest level of annual net bookings and recurrent consumer spending in franchise history [...]

The Grand Theft Auto series also vastly outpaced our forecast with recurrent consumer spending growth of 27%, led by GTA Online's A Safehouse in the Hills update, which featured long-awaited Mansion properties and the return of the fan-favorite protagonist, Michael De Santa. Full game sales of Grand Theft Auto V remains strong with the title now having sold in over 225 million units since its launch in 2013. GTA+ continues to thrive with membership levels nearly doubling over the same period last year, and we're excited about its potential to add even more value to the player experience in the future.

Though it hasn't issued formal guidance yet for FY27, the company is projecting Grand Theft Auto VI will drive record net bookings next year.

Already, even ahead of that launch, Take Two's adjusted EBITDA has roughly doubled, up 97% y/y to $174.8 million (or a 10% margin against bookings).

Key takeaways

Take Two Interactive Software has taken a sharp nosedive over the past few months, and while I acknowledge that the stock remains quite expensive and has a valuation premium to burn off, none of the downside is due to fundamentals. We're encouraged by the company's strong bookings ahead of the highly anticipated release of Take Two's most important franchise, Grand Theft Auto. Add this stock to your watchlist, but don't buy just yet.

data.title: Adobe: The Best Time To Buy Is Often When It Is The Most Difficult
data.description:
In The Midst Of A Historic Drawdown

After researching ServiceNow (NOW) and Trade Desk (TTD) over the last few days, I continue my study of the software industry with Adobe (ADBE), a pioneer in the software-as-a-service (SaaS) business model. Even though it was a highly controversial decision at the time, Adobe was among the first companies to transition from selling perpetual software in a boxed format to offering a cloud-based solution called Adobe Creative Cloud. ADBE unveiled its Creative Cloud subscription service back in April 2012, and it is only now, almost 14 years later, that this model is starting to be questioned by the investing community. If we look into the rear-view mirror, there is no question that Adobe has been operating a world-class SaaS business, but financial markets are forward-looking in nature, and that's where the troubles start.

Adobe's share price reached an all-time high of $699.54 in November 2021, meaning that today's low of ~$255.00 represent a drawdown of more than 60%, which is one of the largest in the company's history. To find a more severe drawdown on Adobe's stock, we have to go back to the global financial crisis of 2008 or to the dot-com bubble that peaked in March 2000. This is how pessimistic market participants have become about the company's growth prospects.

In fact, Adobe is now trading at prices last seen during the depths of the COVID-19 crash, and that is despite much higher revenues and free cash flows. It is also worth noting that in the meantime, the number of diluted shares outstanding went from 485 million at the end of 2020 to 427 million at the end of 2025, a decline of ~12% inclusive of the impacts associated with the large share-based compensation. However, this time around, the worries are not about a major market meltdown triggered by a global pandemic, but about the impacts of artificial intelligence on traditional cloud-based offerings like those offered by Adobe.

As discussed in a previous article, Adobe is far from the only one caught in the middle of a historic drawdown, as it is also the case for pretty much the entire SaaS industry, including names like ServiceNow (NOW), Salesforce (CRM), Intuit (INTU), Workday (WDAY) or Atlassian (TEAM), among many others. The selling has been indiscriminate so far, and I think this is where the opportunity lies. What if high-quality companies like Adobe and ServiceNow can not only adapt, but actually benefit from the rise of artificial intelligence? What if ADBE's offering eventually acts as a platform for AI deployment, instead of being displaced by it?

After reaching an all-time high of $239.62 in January 2025, ServiceNow's share price is currently hovering around $104.00, a drawdown of almost 60%. As illustrated in the chart below, this is the largest decline since the company's initial public offering in June 2012.

Everyone will agree that it has never been easier to create text, images and videos online, as one can simply use artificial intelligence tools like OpenAI's ChatGPT or Anthropic's Claude. It has also become increasingly difficult to distinguish between authentic human content and artificially-generated content. However, equating those facts with the death of Adobe's product suite is quite a stretch, especially knowing how tools like Photoshop, Illustrator or Premiere Pro are deeply embedded in many workflows. There are significant switching costs at play here, starting with compatibility issues as well as the technical knowledge required to be productive using those complex tools. I do not want to minimize the challenges facing Adobe in a world of rapidly growing AI usage, but at the end of the day, I expect Adobe's customer base to be a lot more sticky than what is currently priced by the market right now.

Double-Digit Revenue Growth

For the full-year of 2025, Adobe reported total revenues of $23.8 billion, an increase of 10.5% versus the $21.5 billion reported in the prior year. Looking at the 2026 financial targets provided with the fourth quarter results, total revenues are expected to reach $26.00 billion at the midpoint, which would translate once again into 10%+ top-line growth. On that note, I would like to highlight how impressive it is to achieve double-digit growth on such a large revenue base. This is not what you would expect from a company supposedly facing an existential threat coming from artificial intelligence. Some might say that we are still too early in the game to see a tangible impact on Adobe's financial performance, but given where the stock is currently trading, a slowdown is already priced in. Otherwise, the stock would not be in the middle of a 60%+ drawdown.

Double-Digit Free Cash Flow Yield

In addition to a double-digit revenue growth, Adobe is currently trading at a free cash flow yield in excess of 10%. If I look into my past experience, I think it is quite rare for a company to combine double-digit top-line growth with a double-digit free cash flow yield. In my view, a share price below $260.00 created a highly asymmetric risk-reward ratio for those looking to buy Adobe's stock. As mentioned earlier, the stock is currently in the midst of a historical drawdown, and it is precisely when I like to dip my toes in a new story.

In fact, we only have to rewind back a few years to see that Adobe was widely considered as one of the highest quality compounders out there. When it was trading at its all-time high of almost $600.00 per share, Adobe was the epitome of 'Quality Investing'. Studying Trade Desk (TTD), ServiceNow, and Adobe over the last few days made me realize that quality investing is often synonymous with chasing a narrative. To be clear, I am the first to look for high-quality companies, but quality alone won't lead to a superior investment outcome. This is precisely why I am writing about Adobe today, as the fundamental quality is there, and this is combined with a highly compelling valuation.

As disclosed in Adobe's most recent quarterly results, the company generated operating cash flows of $3.2 billion in the fourth quarter of 2025. Given its capital-light business model, capital expenditures amounted to only $34 million over the same period, which translates into a quarterly free cash flow figure of $3.1 billion. On an annualized basis, and relative to ADBE's current market capitalization of $108.7 billion, this is equivalent to a free cash flow yield of ~11.5%.

With top-line growth expected to be once again into double-digit territory in fiscal 2026, free cash flows should grow further. But even if growth slows down into single-digit territory, it would not be the end of the world with an annualized free cash flow yield well in excess of 10% already. The significant multiple compression that occurred over the last few quarters indicates that growth expectations have cooled off quite a bit, which is the perfect setup for a new buyer like me.

Looking at Adobe's forward price-to-earnings ratio, we can see how it went from ~32.0x in early 2024 to ~11.0x as of right now. As mentioned above, Adobe expects non-GAAP earnings per share of $23.40 in fiscal 2026, which is how we end up with a forward price-to-earnings multiple of ~11.0x. However, if we use the GAAP earnings per share guidance of $18.00, the forward price-to-earnings ratio increases to ~14.4x. The key difference between GAAP and non-GAAP earnings related to share-based compensation, which is a substantial line item not only for Adobe, but for pretty much any SaaS company out there.

Risk Factor

If we go back to Adobe's cash flow statement for 2025, we can see how the company deployed $11.3 billion towards share repurchases. Looking at the income statement, it has translated into a 6% reduction in the number of diluted shares outstanding, from 443 million at the end of November 2024 to 417 million at the end of November 2025. While it is certainly good news to see a constant decline in ADBE's number of shares outstanding, I believe it is important to also include the significant share-based compensation expense in the picture. On one hand, Adobe is aggressively buying back shares, but on the other hand, the effect of those buybacks is in part offset by the issuance of shares to employees and executives. In fiscal 2025 alone, the company's share-based compensation amounted to $1.9 billion, leaving $9.4 billion worth of share repurchases on a net basis.

In my view, the topic of share-based compensation represents a risk not only for Adobe but for the software industry in general, and this is especially true in an environment of rapidly declining share prices. Given its non-cash nature, large share-based compensation expense can significantly overstate the profitability of a company when looking at cash-flow metrics. While non-GAAP financial metrics can highlight the financial performance of the underlying business, looking at GAAP metrics paints a more accurate portrait of Adobe's real profitability.

Another element that I would like to point out is the impact of share-based compensation on employees' morale. When a meaningful proportion of your remuneration comes from stock options, the behaviour of your employer's stock becomes particularly important. When the share price is in a strong uptrend, it works great for everyone. The value of the employees' holdings keeps increasing, and for the employer, it also limits dilution.

However, the opposite is also true when the share price is in the midst of a historic drawdown, as is the case with Adobe today. Employees can see and feel the declining value of their stock holdings, and to offer the same compensation in dollars, the employer must also issue more shares, translating into higher dilution for existing shareholders. This is the situation in which Adobe is finding itself in today, hence why I view share-based compensation as a risk.

Conclusion

Adobe is currently in the midst of one of its largest-ever share price drawdowns. Similar to many other software names I have previously covered, this is primarily due to fears related to AI disruption. In ADBE's particular case, I think those fears are now overstated, as I would expect the company's customer base to remain more sticky than what is currently implied by market prices.

Generally, Adobe Creative Cloud is deeply-imbedded in customers' workflow, and switching to a new offering is not as easy as it sounds. Moreover, artificial intelligence is now an integral part of Adobe's offering, including multiple new Photoshop functionalities, for example. In this context, I view Adobe as a key enabler for artificial intelligence instead of a target for disruption.

Based on the company's most recent guidance, top-line growth is expected to remain in double-digit territory in fiscal 2026. This is accompanied by a net cash balance sheet and an annualized free cash flow yield of ~11.5% excluding the impact of share-based compensation. Even if we include $2.0 billion in share-based compensation expense, the free cash flow yield remains substantial at approximately 9.8%.

How many times do we have the opportunity to buy a company with a double-digit free cash flow yield, a double-digit top-line growth and a net cash balance sheet? The 'Strong Buy' rating reflects my view that the current share price below $260.00 creates a highly asymmetric risk-reward ratio. Nobody knows the future and the ultimate impact artificial intelligence will have on our lives. However, what I do know is that the lower Adobe's share price goes, the higher the probability of success becomes. The best times to buy are often when it is the most difficult to do so.

data.title: ClearBridge Emerging Markets Strategy Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Semiconductor Strength Drives EM Outperformance

Market and Performance Overview

Emerging markets advanced 4.7% in the fourth quarter to finish the year as one of the best-performing global equity asset classes. The benchmark MSCI Emerging Markets Index rose 33.6% for 2025, outpacing the developed market MSCI EAFE Index (+31.2%) and handily outperforming the U.S. S&P 500 Index. (+17.9%).

Within the benchmark, contribution to performance in the fourth quarter was led by Korea and Taiwan, which offset weakness in China. Korean equities soared 27.3% in the quarter, led by a 50% gain for its information technology (IT) sector, while AI-related momentum helped Taiwanese stocks climb 10.4%. Strength in precious metals lifted South Africa 14.1%, while India — rebounding from weak performance throughout 2025 — performed in line with the benchmark. China, meanwhile, declined 7.4% due to profit taking among its largest technology companies after a strong first three quarters of the year, as well as continued softness in consumer spending. Another notable detractor was Saudi Arabia (-7.6%) due to falling oil prices and lingering negative sentiment toward the Middle East.

Exhibit 1: Emerging Markets Outperformed U.S. in 2025

Data as of Dec. 31, 2025. Source: FactSet.

From a sector standpoint, AI-related stocks continued to thrive, lifting IT to a 16.4% gain, while the materials sector advanced 11.6% as gold prices rose more than 10% during the quarter and copper prices hit record highs. The energy, financials and industrials sectors also outperformed the index, while consumer discretionary, communication services, health care and real estate companies underperformed.

The ClearBridge Emerging Markets Strategy outperformed the benchmark on both a quarterly and full-year basis, boosted by strength among our IT holdings. Korean semiconductor maker SK Hynix (HXSC.F) soared more than 80% in the fourth quarter and nearly tripled in price for the year on accelerating demand for its DRAM and NAND memory, particularly high-bandwidth memory products used for AI workloads. Shares of Korean memory competitor Samsung Electronics (SSNLF) were also up strongly over both periods as memory shortages enabled stronger pricing power. Both companies are among the Strategy’s top five holdings. Our largest position, Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM), benefited from its position as the leading manufacturer of high-end chips for Nvidia (NVDA) and other top semiconductor designers, also meaningfully outperforming the benchmark for both the quarter and the year.

Exhibit 2: Global DRAM Market Dominated by Korea

Data as of March 31, 2025. Source: Counterpoint.

Other significant contributors included China’s Sieyuan Electric and Korea’s HD Hyundai Electric, industrial holdings supporting AI data center buildouts and power requirements, as East Asia continues to gain share in global AI supply chains. Capitec Bank, a retail banking and insurance provider, delivered strong financial results amid a supportive environment in South Africa, and Titan, an Indian jewelry retailer, produced strong results with bullish comments from management in its latest quarterly release. We view Titan’s performance as encouraging for an Indian market that significantly underperformed the broader benchmark in 2025.

Alibaba (BABA) and Tencent (TCEHY) , two of the largest names in the Chinese equity market, surrendered gains as part of a broader rotation out of China after strong performance earlier in the year. Chinese battery supplier Contemporary Amperex Technology (CTATF)(CATL) was also down, with its high active weight in the portfolio weighing on relative performance. However, we believe that each of these companies remains fundamentally sound and well positioned as the world’s second largest economy works through a growth slowdown.

Latin America e-commerce platform MercadoLibre (MELI) remained weak due to concerns over the investment level needed to fend off increasing competition in Brazil. Apollo Hospitals and ICICI Bank (IBN) also detracted due to their large active portfolio weights in an Indian market still trailing the broader strength across emerging markets.

Portfolio Positioning

During the quarter, the Strategy added four new positions and closed out of three others.

Brazil was a focus of activity and included new positions in pharmacy operator Raia Drogasil (RADLY) and fintech company Nu Holdings (NU). Raia Drogasil is a high-quality company looking to reaccelerate revenue after investing in the business, features a high return on capital and is a primary beneficiary of GLP-1 demand in Brazil. Nu Holdings, which has become one of the major banking disruptors in South America through its offerings of digital banking, credit and prepaid cards, is driving long-term growth through expansion of products and markets. Meanwhile we exited our position in stock exchange B3 S.A. - Brasil, Bolsa, Balcão, which has been a beneficiary of increasing trading. Capitalizing on its strong returns in 2025, we elected to exit the position and redeploy assets into areas we believe have greater return potential.

We upgraded our Indonesia exposure, replacing PT Bank Rakyat Indonesia (BKRKY) with PT Bank Central Asia (PBCRF), Indonesia’s leading non-state-owned bank. Rakyat’s micro business, its most profitable segment, has faced asset quality and growth challenges that we believe will take longer to recover than is currently embedded in market expectations, which would pressure the business’ profitability and returns. PT Bank Central Asia, meanwhile, has the best deposit franchise in Indonesia, and we took advantage of a selloff in the region over the last year to purchase this high-quality business at an attractive valuation.

We also initiated a position in Harbin Electric in the industrials sector. We believe that power generation is a key global theme and China will play a significant role. Nuclear is a re-emerging area of investment and Harbin is a key supplier of equipment to this industry. We expect that the company will grow revenues and increase its profitability for a long-term, sustained period.

Our other sale was Chinese consumer staples company Proya Cosmetics. After previously benefiting from market share gains in its home market, Proya has seen deterioration in the growth of its business due to increased competition from other global cosmetics and skin care brands.

Outlook

Following a protracted period of relative underperformance versus developed markets, we believe the EM market recovery is at an early stage. Valuations are appealing, global macroeconomic drivers are supportive and local structural and company-level opportunities all point toward significant upside potential for the asset class.

International asset flows are also critical in driving prices for EM markets. The opportunity offered by lower valuations, combined with stronger economic growth and improving investor sentiment, creates a virtuous cycle attracting increased foreign capital flows. This, in turn, further enhances potential investment performance. We are still at an early stage in this process and anticipate increased foreign investments into EM equities over the coming years. We are bullish on three major themes in the year ahead: China, technology and India.

The Chinese economy has begun to demonstrate increased stability, with trade tensions easing, exports remaining strong and a generally more optimistic outlook emerging. Our earlier belief that China wouldn’t separate much from the global economy has proven true, as relationships have improved and investors have regained confidence.

EM offers the opportunity to invest in world-class companies with cutting-edge technological innovation, with many of these companies benefiting from substantial investment in research and development and intellectual property creation. EM tech is best known for providing exposure to key global supply chain components for tech hardware. Tech investment can be found across industries; we see opportunities in areas such as industrial automation, e-commerce and fintech, as well as technological advancement driving increased global power demand amid the growth of AI data centers and wider electrification of the economy.

2025 was a challenging year for Indian equities after a sharp valuation pullback following the 2024 election, negative economic growth revisions and consumer softness. However, the long-term investment case remains robust: India offers great upside potential as it benefits from a large and young population, and it remains the fastest-growing major global economy, with real GDP growth of 6.6% compared to 4.2% for EM overall.

Portfolio Highlights

The ClearBridge Emerging Market Strategy outperformed its MSCI Emerging Markets benchmark in the fourth quarter. On an absolute basis, the Strategy produced gains across five of the nine sectors in which it was invested (out of 11 total). The primary contributor was the IT sector while the main detractors were consumer discretionary and communication services.

Relative to the benchmark, overall stock selection and sector allocation contributed to performance. In particular, selection in the IT and industrials sectors and an overweight to IT drove results. Conversely, selection in communication services and an underweight to materials detracted from performance.

On a regional basis, stock selection in Korea, China, Taiwan and Brazil and an underweight to China contributed to performance while selection in Indonesia and Mexico proved detrimental.

On an individual stock basis, the leading contributors to relative performance were SK Hynix, Samsung Electronics, Sieyuan Electric, Capitec Bank and not holding Xiaomi. The primary detractors were Tencent, Alibaba, CATL, MercadoLibre and Apollo Hospitals.
