data.title: PLTW's Weekly Cash Flow Comes At A Cost
data.description:
Roundhill PLTR WeeklyPay ETF (PLTW) uses Palantir's (PLTR) volatility to fund a high weekly cash distribution. It does not use option premiums, nor does it generate income from Palantir's cash flows. In this way, PLTW is an income fund that's very different from traditional dividend or option-income funds.

PLTW uses a rules-based structure, and it uses high leverage that resets every week. The cash they pay out depends on market volatility, so it must be evaluated alongside capital behavior and path dependency. By that I mean, PLTW can give you strong cash flow, but only under certain price conditions, and often at the cost of NAV over a period of time. In this article we examine how PLTW works, where its distributions come from, and if it has any place in a disciplined, income-focused portfolio.

How PLTW Works

PLTW is designed to provide ~120% of the calendar-week total return of PLTR, before fees and expenses. This resets every week - as defined in the fund's prospectus dated February 18, 2025 - and is key to understanding how the fund behaves over time.

PLTW does not primarily hold PLTR shares, but uses total return swaps to try to achieve the 120% return. PLTW allocates at least 80% of its net assets to swaps and/or equity. The rest is held in short-term UST-bills, Treasury ETFs, money market funds, and cash to collateralize those derivatives.

Investors will understand that holding PLTW is not the same as holding PLTR. They do not get Palantir dividends, voting rights, and so on.

Now, once the calendar week is over, the entire PLTW exposure is rebalanced back to the 120% target. Thus, if PLTR goes up sharply in one week, exposure gets reduced into the next week - and vice versa. This specific reset mechanism introduces path dependency. That means, it isn't just Palantir's long-term direction that drives outcomes but also its weekly price move sequences.

There is no promised fixed payout with PLTW. The entire weekly cash distribution is formulaic, and variable. So they depend on recent price performance and market volatility. As we see from recent Rule 19a-1 notices, much of the recent distribution amounts have come from return of capital, rather than net investment income. Indeed, for the latest distribution, 100% have come from ROC.

This structure works well when Palantir shows persistent volatility but with a sideways or upward movement. It doesn't work so well during either extended drawdowns or prolonged low-volatility periods. Those conditions accelerate NAV erosion. A simple numerical example will illustrate this for four distinct scenarios. Here, I have assumed that all scenarios start with a PLTW NAV of $100, the weekly leverage target is 120% of PLTR's weekly return, and the weekly distribution is $2. I have ignored fees for clarity, and have assumed all distributions to be entirely ROC.

As this table shows, higher and sequential volatility works better for the fund than even PLTR's upward movement. On the surface, Scenario D looks better than A, but compared to the underlying stock's growth path, PLTW performs poorly in Scenario D.

Return of Capital: Benign or destructive?

As we just saw, in recent weeks, a large portion of PLTW's distributions have been classified as ROC. In fact, the most recent Rule 19a-1 notice shows the distribution to be 100% ROC. This sounds concerning, but ROC by itself does not automatically mean the fund is "destroying value."

Broadly, return of capital is often a tax classification. When distribution comes neither from taxable NII nor realized capital gains, what's left to fund the distribution is ROC. In PLTW's derivatives-based setup, economic gains can happen without being realized or taxable in the same period. This is usually why distributions get labeled as ROC even when clear economic value has been generated.

That said, there are two very different forms of ROC, and the distinction is critical. There's a benign ROC, where things happen just as I described above. The fund does generate economic value, but a simple tax classification process labels distribution as ROC. Here, the cost basis gets reduced, but NAV does not get hurt by the so-called ROC. ROC is basically a tax deferral mechanism here. It is not capital erosion.

Then there's destructive ROC, where the fund does not generate enough economic return to support distribution. So they pay your distribution using your own capital. NAV declines, future earnings capacity is reduced.

When it comes to PLTW, it can do either, depending on how Palantir's price path is looking in a particular period. As my table above shows, when PLTR is volatile, PLTW's NAV can get replenished, making ROC largely benign. At other times - all of the other 3 scenarios - ROC causes NAV erosion.

Therefore, PLTW's ROC should be judged in conjunction with the fund's capital dynamics and the market environment in which it operates. It should not be assessed in isolation. The same goes for the distribution as well.

Swap Mechanics and Why the Outcomes Look the Way They Do

PLTW does not actually have to own PLTR; it uses total return swaps (TRS), a contract through which PLTW receives/pays the full price return of PLTR against a specified notional reference. In exchange for not needing to own PLTR, it pays a financing cost to the swap counterparty.

Here's a numerical example that explains this stuff.

Let's say PLTW begins a certain week with $100 of NAV. It targets 120% weekly exposure. It does not buy $120 of PLTR stock. Instead, it enters into a swap with a $120 notional reference to PLTR. So we get two scenarios:

  • Scenario 1 - PLTR rises 5% during the week. The total return on the swap notional is +$6 (5% × $120). The counterparty pays PLTW $6, and NAV rises to $106 before any distribution.

  • Scenario 2 - PLTR falls 5%, the total return is −$6, and PLTW pays the counterparty $6, reducing NAV to $94 before any distribution.

On top of this, PLTW pays a financing rate on the swap notional. This is usually tied to short-term interest rates plus a spread - PLTW is basically borrowing money here. This financing cost may appear modest on a weekly basis, but when there's low volatility, it could become a lot.

We also discussed path dependency, where the weekly reset reduces exposure when there's a gain and increases exposure when there's a loss.

I presented a table of data showing some numerical scenarios, earlier. Those 4 scenarios are nothing but the math that happens when you combine the swap structure with the weekly distribution. For example, in my volatile+sideways scenario, a +5% week produces a +$6 swap gain, more than enough to fund a $2 distribution while also adding to NAV. However, in a low-volatility+sideways market, a +1% move produces only a +$1.20 swap gain, which is insufficient to fund the same $2 distribution. That erodes your PLTW NAV even when PLTR is broadly flat.

Where PLTW fits

PLTW is not for everyone - that should be obvious from the discussion above. It is not for people interested in long-term equity compounding, people looking for stable income streams, or people who cannot be bothered by high volatility portfolios. PLTW is not at all for income investors looking for "set-and-forget" holdings.

Rather, PLTW is a tactical income tool not to grow capital, but to extract cash from short-term, highly volatile price movements. It works—at times—in the hands of active investors. These are folks looking for current cash flows, not capital growth. You must be able to digest volatility, use it to monetize your capital. Most important, PLTW is not a core position. It should be nothing but a fraction of the entire portfolio.

I rate PLTW a Hold. The structure can work when there's enough volatility in PLTR, and if the user is disciplined. However, it does not offer durable returns, so I cannot call it an unequivocal buy. Nor can I call it a blanket sell because it works for some people some of the time. There is nothing inherently broken - it is just that PLTW is not for most income investors, including this author.

data.title: Ondas: Expensive, Dilutive, Unprofitable - And Still A Buy
data.description:
Ondas Inc. (ONDS) is in a phase of transition from R&D to early commercial traction, backed by recent funding that improves its runway significantly. At this stage of growth, rapid growth revisions are a very bullish sign, and inroads in autonomous and counter-drone use cases are promising and sticky once established. That profile makes it a great growth bet with a high-risk, high-reward setup.

I feel the focus now should be on the next few quarters showing further traction and revenue upgrades. On that front, what we have to date looks good for a risk-aware Buy call. Hence, I am willing to underwrite profitability concerns that should only become a discussion point after installations scale (not a 2026 topic).

Valuation Discussion Early - Monitor Growth

With an EV of ~$3.68b and 2026 revenue targets of $175m at the midpoint, the forward EV to revenue ratio works out to be ~21x. This may screen as expensive whichever way we look at it. Seeking Alpha notes the sector median as ~3.2x, but the range is expectedly high today in defense and drone stocks, so it is difficult to pinpoint the level of potential excess here.

Legacy defense stocks often have EV to forward revenue of 2-3x (say Lockheed Martin). Mid-tier high-growth drone companies (say AeroVironment) command valuations even higher – upward of 5x EV to forward revenue. High growth tech (like Kratos) can command even higher multiples in double-digit EV-to-forward-revenue ratio terms.

The biggest question that helps the Ondas Buy thesis is how well the premium is supported by a tangible platform developed and how strong the initial moat signals are. And that answers growth potential alongside the valuation premiums (a lesser concern in early-stage defense tech). Ondas is not a single product drone developer; it is assembling a multi-layer defense system spanning counter-UAS (for detection and interception), autonomous ISR drones, ground robotics, and a command and control layer for the system. This integrated solution position increases switching costs once embedded. Contracts and deployments are also usually sticky, plus there are regulatory and onboarding hurdles that new entrants face. That is the kind of moat that matters a lot in defense tech. The counter-drone protection, autonomous surveillance, and robotics offerings from Ondas are in early adoption trends in defense today – vendor positions are not yet locked. As Ondas lands and proves its worth, it can easily expand, and that in itself can create a moat enabling Ondas to scale further into multi-year programs.

However, the bigger question right now is traction and initial signals – how well is Ondas landing? There are some encouraging early signs. The Roboteam acquisition opens up a recurring and repeat order stream for ground robots in US military units and Israeli defense forces. One line also has graduated to a program-of-record status, indicating a high probability of follow-through procurement, refreshes, and multi-year support revenue. The robotics segment is closest to traditional defense procurement trends and also is relatively embedded already (initial deployments). The counter-UAS (Iron Drone and Sentrycs) deployments appear to be multi-site and increasing in coverage per site across airports and military bases. Progress in Europe appears strong. Ondas is also winning multi-year sovereign programs, signaling a move from pilots into repeatable government deployments.

The demand signal and growth positioning are even more apparent looking at the fast-paced upgrades Ondas is going through at the moment. From mid-November to the end of December 2025, the backlog has grown from ~$23m to ~$65m. Even the revenue outlook has been upgraded significantly within a matter of a few months. In Q2, Ondas had a target revenue of ~$25m for the full year. By Q3, the target was raised to $36m. 2026 targets have also moved up in no time. In Q3, 2026 targets were $110m. Within two months, during the Analyst Day call, this was revised upward to $175 at the midpoint.

The backlog strength and meaningful upward revisions not only support a positive outlook in terms of traction and demand strength, but they also show valuations at this early-stage hypergrowth phase could be misleading. A 50% revenue revision is happening in just a quarter. If the revenue is further upgraded from $170m by 50%, the EV to forward revenue ratio immediately falls to ~14x. The initial years of revenue growth are particularly capable of allowing the share price to grow into more reasonable valuations. After some scale is achieved and base numbers are formed, valuations could become a more important gauge of how pricey Ondas is. For now, all focus should be on revenue upgrades and inroads - and fully expecting some pullbacks in targets and realized revenue too, given the lumpiness of contract signings. And there the recent revenue trajectory is fully supportive of a risk-aware Buy. The path to what could be more reasonable high-growth multiples is also within reach (early teens EV to forward revenue).

Concerns around Profitability and Dilution

Ondas's thesis does have two drags that should be acknowledged. Margins are obviously a worry, but they will remain so given that Ondas is in a build mode. The gross margins chart does not show current levels as unprecedented, but combined with revenue growth that has picked up since 2024, gross margins have improved from major negative territory to ~10% now. It is now stagnating around these levels, but Ondas is still in a phase where upfront installation costs, manufacturing readiness, and even integration costs from acquisitions are expected to weigh on profitability. Ondas has a long-term target of 50%+ gross margins, and this is still a metric to watch in 2026; even a path to green or minor improvements will be supportive. EBITDA and operating profitability come in much later as scale kicks in. As of now, I don't see margins at a stage worrying enough to block my Buy call - improvements have been made from 2024, and as manufacturing scale efficiencies show up, integration costs come down, and the mix stabilizes, we could see visible improvements in 2026 itself.

Another concern that is usually analyzed for a manufacturing-heavy and early-stage company like Ondas is dilution. Dilution has been significant, no doubt. Share count has increased by 3-4x in 2025 alone. And there is potentially significant overhang left in the form of warrants too. As of September 30, 2025, parent-level warrant overhang was modest (~2.6m shares). However, the October 2025 financing introduced ~73.9m additional common warrants (and ~17.4m pre-funded warrant share equivalents), materially increasing potential dilution. As per the transaction details outlined in the 10-Q filing:

On October 6, 2025, the Company entered into an underwriting agreement (the “October 2025 Underwriting Agreement”) with Oppenheimer & Co. Inc., as representative of the several underwriters (the “October Underwriters”), relating to the Company’s underwritten offering (the “October 2025 Offering”) of (i) 19,560,000 shares (the “October Shares”) of Common Stock, or (ii) in lieu of Common Stock, pre-funded warrants (the “October Pre-Funded Warrants,” together with the October Shares, the “October Common Stock Equivalents”) to purchase up to 17,400,000 shares of Common Stock (the “October Pre-Funded Warrant Shares”). The October Common Stock Equivalents were accompanied by warrants (the “October Common Warrants,” together with the October Pre-Funded Warrants, the “October Warrants”) to purchase a total of 73,920,000 shares of Common Stock (the “October Common Warrant Shares,” together with the October Pre-Funded Warrant Shares, the “October Warrant Shares”).

On a pro forma basis, fully diluted share count could exceed ~450m shares if these instruments are exercised. That is significant but does not invalidate the Buy thesis as long as revenue traction continues to outpace the expanding share count.

So that remains a risk to the thesis. However, with a cash balance of ~$450m now and $26m cash used for the first three quarters of 2025, we are easily looking at a multi-year runway. The pressure for survival capital raises is definitely diminished today. Growth capital raises could still pressure dilution-led valuation concerns, but as long as growth keeps showing up, the thesis remains intact. Which brings us back to the single pillar that Ondas' speculative Buy thesis hinges on – that is, growth alone, at this stage. Shares outstanding have roughly quadrupled, but revenue targets have increased nearly 7x in the same period – the investment case hinges on that gap continuing to widen.

A Speculative Buy with an Eye on Growth

Overall, the Ondas thesis leans entirely on 2026 growth. If that remains on track, risks of gross margins stagnating well below targets or further non-growth-oriented dilution decrease. Which means the current trend of significant upward revisions to revenue targets must continue. That may not happen right away in the upcoming Q4 results (given sales cycle lumpiness), but over 2026, two things should happen clearly. One, Ondas should show actual revenues filling in the $175m midpoint target for the year. And two, revisions to the 2026 target should be sizeable enough to overcome dilution concerns and bring valuations to a more reasonable low double-digit level. For that to happen, we are looking for revenue targets of ~$250 through 2026. Or a bigger outlook for the next financial year, 2027.

In the upcoming Q4 data, I will look for significant beats to the revenue guidance of ~$28m at the midpoint (as guided during the Analyst Day in January). An exit revenue run rate of ~$35m annualizes to ~$140m for 2026 and covers a lot of ground in meeting expectations. This is not entirely a blue-sky ask. In Q3, revenue beat was around 30%, although off a lower base. Also, a miss there does not invalidate the thesis immediately. What matters more is indications from the commentary that show continued backlog growth and commercial demand signals for the upcoming quarters. As of now, backlog looks healthy, and revenue visibility is decent. So, I will recommend entries today with enough buffer cash to top up positions if Q4 disappoints and/or Ondas pulls back meaningfully without any real harm done to the future growth story.

data.title: ClearBridge Large Cap Value Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: U.S. equities finished a strong 2025, albeit with more subdued gains in the fourth quarter, with the S&P 500 Index rising 2.7% during the period to end the year up 17.9%. Value stocks outpaced growth in the quarter across all market caps as the market pivoted from recent high-momentum AI winners and leadership broadened. The benchmark Russell 1000 Value Index gained 3.8% for the quarter, capping off a 15.9% return for 2025.
  • Contributors: Stock selection in financials and health care, underweights to real estate and consumer staples and a health care overweight were beneficial.
  • Detractors: Stock selection in the IT, materials and consumer discretionary sectors detracted.
  • Outlook: Thanks to our long duration, we see the recent selloff in high-quality businesses as a significant opportunity, and we have recently initiated or added to positions in several at attractive valuations.

Performance Review

  • During the fourth quarter, quality stocks across the market cap spectrum underperformed the broader market by the widest margin since the late 1990s. This was a headwind to the Strategy, which trailed the Russell 1000 Value in the quarter as several high-quality long-term holdings sold off.
  • Within information technology (IT), long-term portfolio holding Motorola Solutions sold off as its revenue growth rate continued to normalize in its core land mobile radio (LMR) devices business; the company is working off an unusually high backlog created by COVID-era government funding programs. While its LMR devices business is slowing, we think the company has augmented its core franchise with faster-growing adjacencies and increased its mix of software and services. We think Motorola Solutions (MSI) is a core holding for our strategy that offers attractive risk-reward at current valuation given a sustainable mid-single-digit top-line growth rate with continuously improving profit margins.
  • Our high-quality bias applies across the portfolio, including areas of AI. We are more exposed to higher-quality AI-exposed stocks such as Broadcom (AVGO), Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) and Microsoft (MSFT), which lagged this year (although the first two were both up more than 40%) relative to lower-quality more commodity-exposed AI stocks like Micron (MU), Sandisk (SNDK) and Lumentum (LITE). While these lower-quality companies are likely to benefit more from supply chain constraints in the near term, we believe that businesses with more sustainable moats are likely to deliver superior returns through the cycle for investors.
  • Strength in consumer spending from the third quarter looked to carry over into the holiday season, helping Capital One (COF) and American Express (AXP). Capital One reported a large earnings beat driven by lower credit costs and a large reserve release helped by lower charge-offs. The quarter showed very strong operating results from Capital One in its first full quarter following its acquisition of Discovery. The icing on the cake was the announcement of a $16 billion buyback authorization (11%–12% of the market cap) following management's review of post-acquisition capital requirements. American Express also topped earnings estimates and raised the lower end of its guidance helped by strong spending and a bounce back in travel. Its refresh on its Platinum cards is also being well-received.
  • A fourth-quarter rotation to health care driven by a decline in policy uncertainty, improved earnings, attractive valuations and a pickup in M&A activity helped our holdings in the sector.
  • Thermo Fisher Scientific (TMO), a supplier of life science tools and diagnostics, delivered strong earnings driven by its analytical instruments segment and strong operating margins. The company has also benefited from improving results in its growing bioprocessing business and raised revenue and EPS guidance.
  • Strength in its oncology segment helped AstraZeneca (AZN) deliver stronger-than-expected third-quarter results in November and reaffirm its guidance.

Outlook

  • Our core tenet is to invest in high-quality businesses with underappreciated, sustainable, durable edges, and we remain convinced that this is the path to superior returns through the cycle. In the current frothy market, however, this style has been under pressure. Thanks to our long duration, we see the recent selloff in high-quality businesses as a significant opportunity, and we have recently initiated or added to positions in several — such as Linde (LIN), Parker-Hannifin (PH) and WEC Energy (WEC) — at attractive valuations. We believe the shifting market regime offers significant value opportunities, especially as overall market breadth improves and capital flows shift away from high-flying growth stocks toward overlooked, high-quality businesses.

Principal value and investment returns will fluctuate, and investors' shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than the original cost. Performance would have been lower if fees had not been waived in various periods. Total returns assume the reinvestment of all distributions and the deduction of all fund expenses. Returns with sales charge reflect a deduction of the stated maximum sales charge. An investor cannot invest directly in an index, and unmanaged index returns do not reflect any fees, expenses or sales charges. Returns for periods of less than one year are not annualized. All classes of shares may not be available to all investors or through all distribution channels. For current month-end performance, please visit Mutual Funds | ETFs | Insights.

What are the Risks?

All investments involve risks, including possible loss of principal. Equity securities are subject to price fluctuation and possible loss of principal. International investments are subject to special risks, including currency fluctuations and social, economic and political uncertainties, which could increase volatility. These risks are magnified in emerging markets. Short selling is a speculative strategy. Unlike the possible loss on a security that is purchased, there is no limit on the amount of loss on an appreciating security that is sold short. Derivative instruments can be illiquid, may disproportionately increase losses, and have a potentially large impact on performance. The investment style may become out of favor, which may have a negative impact on performance. In addition, the manager may not assess every investment for ESG criteria, and not every ESG factor may be identified or evaluated. These and other risks are discussed in the fund's prospectus.

A buyback is the repurchase of outstanding shares (repurchase) by a company in order to reduce the number of shares on the market.

Duration is a measure of the sensitivity of a bond's price to changes in interest rates.

Earnings per share (EPS) is defined as a company's net income divided by the number of outstanding shares of its common stock.

Moat is an advantage a company has which allows it to protect its market share and profitability.

data.title: Trading In My Military Uniform For The Markets: My Transition From The Navy To Seeking Alpha
data.description:
Introduction

When I joined the U.S. Navy at 18-years-old, two months out of high school, I honestly didn't know what I was getting myself into at the time.

Before joining, I planned to do the same as many Americans. Go to college, work a traditional job, retire in my 60s, and ride off into the sunset.

After a family member suggested I try the military, I took and passed the ASVAB test, signed on the dotted line, and the rest is history. My career flowed by, and after about 10 years, I realized I was in it for the long haul.

After visiting various countries , several duty stations, seven U.S. warships, multiple deployments, and 21 years later, I decided it was time to retire.

In this article, I discuss my transition from the military to Seeking Alpha, what got me into investing, and my advice to new contributors on how to succeed on the platform.

How My Military Service Shaped Me for the Transition

During my time in the military, I learned a lot of things about life. However, finance wasn't one of them. My career in the Navy as a Boatswain's Mate was the farthest thing from finance.

But lessons from visiting multiple countries, experiencing different cultures, and seeing my fellow veterans struggle post-retirement is what initially piqued my interest in finance.

So, I started reading investment magazines. And anything related to Warren Buffett.

After completing my first two deployments and saving a bit of money, I was stationed in Texas.

This is where I first dipped my toes into investing. I gained knowledge from my chief, as he often mentioned taking a simple approach by investing in no-load, no-fee mutual funds.

He also mentioned buying and holding blue-chip stocks like The Coca-Cola Company (KO), PepsiCo (PEP), and Procter & Gamble (PG).

Although I would listen and often invest, I didn't have the mental capacity to actually become a successful investor.

It wasn't until a few years before retirement that I actually took investing very seriously. The next chapter of my life was rapidly approaching, and I wanted to avoid what many face after the military - financial struggles.

Approaching Retirement Made Me Take Investing Serious

After getting stationed in California at 18 years in, I figured it was time to take investing more seriously. It was there I started researching and soaking in as much knowledge as I could.

Taking all the lessons from Warren Buffett and my chief about buying and holding quality investments for the long term, this is where dividends came in.

Although most view dividend investing as boring and tax inefficient since dividends are double taxed, I saw it the opposite way. Who wouldn't want to collect passive income for simply buying and holding an investment?

Stumbling Upon Seeking Alpha

Becoming very interested in dividends, I often researched dividend stocks. I'm still not quite sure how I stumbled upon Seeking Alpha, but it was most likely from a Google search.

Shortly after discovering the site in 2021, I was hooked. I would spend countless hours after work and on the weekends reading articles.

I didn't want to end up like most of my fellow veterans. Having to go right back to work after retirement from a lack of planning.

I wanted dividends to supplement my income in retirement to alleviate the pressure of having to work full time. After being on Seeking Alpha, I was introduced to REITs (XLRE) and business development companies (BIZD).

This is how I found authors like Brad Thomas and Hoya Capital. Fast forward a few years later, and I never thought I would be asked to work with them in an Investing Group.

Brad and I co-authored quite a few articles before his transition away from the platform. I've even had the chance to co-author a few REIT-focused articles with Cash Flow Venue as well.

REITs and BDCs are distant cousins that were both created by Congress. By law they're required to pay out 90% of their taxable income in the form of dividends. Because of this, they typically have higher yields.

The higher the yield, the more income you can collect now. But like many investors, I'll admit I got caught up in chasing high yield.

After getting burned by Medical Properties Trust (MPT), highly touted here on Seeking Alpha a few years ago, I decided it was time to make a change to my investment strategy.

Instead of focusing primarily on yield, I shifted to quality. While quality stocks typically have lower yields, these usually have strong cash flows and can continue rewarding shareholders with dividend increases.

Buying quality usually leads to capital appreciation instead of capital loss, like lower-quality, higher-yielding investments. This also is what provoked me to become a Seeking Alpha analyst as well.

With retirement approaching and entertaining job offers, I decided to give it a try in May 2023, five months before my actual retirement from the U.S. Navy. After working with the editors, I finally published my first article titled The Dividend Collectuh's 18 Stock Tim Duncan Portfolio.

My goal on Seeking Alpha was simple: To help others. I wanted to do this by taking a simple approach to investing in dividend stocks and always giving my honest opinion.

I usually only analyze stocks and ETFs that I consider high quality. Companies with strong cash flows, growing earnings, and staying power.

Almost three years, close to 800 articles, and approximately 8,500 followers later, my success on Seeking Alpha has turned me from a military retiree to a full-time analyst.

My Success on Seeking Alpha

My transition from the military to Seeking Alpha has been quite fun. I honestly didn't expect to be here nearly three years later. Or that I would be working in an Investing Group with people that I looked up to on the platform.

I remember being new here and reaching out to a few successful analysts whose articles I enjoyed reading; one who I actually talked to was Julian Lin. I reached out for advice on how to be successful on Seeking Alpha.

One of the things I remember he said was to be honest and consistent. This was easy for me, as I had spent 21 years serving in the Navy. Everything was about honor, courage, and commitment. And also consistency.

My Advice to Other Contributors

If I were to give advice to new contributors on Seeking Alpha, or anyone looking to become one, I would say always be honest with your analysis. And to be consistent. These two things are what I believe helped me grow the following I have so far.

My main goal since starting on Seeking Alpha has remained the same. Help others. I've always said if my articles help at least one person make a better investment decision, then I'm happy.

Another thing I suggest is to be open to learning from others. I can't tell you how much I've learned from readers in the comment section. I'm always being introduced to new stocks and funds or gaining a new perspective on the overall market.

That's why I feel it's so important to engage with your readers, even when comments are critical of your analysis. You can always learn something. Taking this approach, it's highly likely you will not only grow your knowledge but also a decent following as well.

Conclusion

Being a reader here on Seeking Alpha has expanded my investment knowledge. Becoming an analyst, working with editors, and engaging with readers and other analysts has only helped me.

Joining the military at such a young age gave me an advantage. This allowed me to successfully retire and transition to Seeking Alpha as a full-time analyst.

And after nearly three years, I've enjoyed my tenure while vastly expanding my investment knowledge. Writing on this platform has shaped my (investment) strategy throughout the years. If you're a new analyst or someone looking to become one, don't be afraid to start.
